one_line_takeaway: Not an AI-security paper: it is an interpretability/formal-verification study showing that chess-playing GPT-2-style sequence models never achieve soundness (i.e., always have moves in their training/output distribution that produce illegal continuations under an adversarial legal-move search), and that linear board-state probes are only weakly causally connected to next-move prediction despite tracking board state accurately.
key_finding: None of the 24 trained chess models are sound: for most models at least one adversary (almost always IMO) achieves a success rate near 100% (Table 1, e.g., IMO reaches 0.999-1.000 on Millionbase-500K and Random-2M configs, and still 0.972-0.992 on the largest Random-10M models). Even models with very high raw legal-move test accuracy (99.75%-99.98% for Random-10M/Stockfish-8M/Lichess-16M) are shown to be unsound once evaluated with a strong adversary such as IMO, versus only 94.65%-96.71% legal-move ratio for the smaller Random-500K/Millionbase-500K models. IMO is consistently the strongest attack by a wide margin; the AD attack (reimplementing Vafa et al. 2024) performs similarly to the Random-Move baseline (e.g., AD=0.516 vs RM=0.673 on Random-10M/NT, and both far below IMO=0.972), indicating that low-probability-move steering is a weak verification signal compared to explicitly targeting the model's blind spots for illegal continuations. BSO (targeting the board-state probe's error) is sometimes even weaker than the non-adversarial SMM baseline, e.g., on Lichess-16M/NT: BSO=0.240 vs SMM=0.623. Larger training datasets substantially increase robustness to attack independent of dataset type/objective (Figure 1); models trained on curated (non-random) datasets with the plain NT objective appear especially robust when data is abundant (e.g., Lichess-16M/NT: IMO=0.654) but Section 7 shows this apparent soundness is an artifact of staying in-distribution: when evaluated from out-of-distribution warmup prefixes, IMO success rate on Lichess-16M/NT rises from 0.654 to 0.667/0.785/0.864 at 20/40/60 OOD moves respectively (Table 4), i.e., +0.28 to +0.48 absolute increase. Joint board-state-probe training (+JP objective) yields negligible soundness improvement. Gradient-alignment analysis (Table 2) finds the next-token-prediction loss gradient and the board-state-probe loss gradient are nearly orthogonal (cosine distance 0.966-1.000 across all 24 models), indicating the probe head and the generation head rely on largely independent subspaces of the representation, contrary to the hypothesis motivating joint-probe training. Table 3 shows that for large datasets the fraction of BSO-induced illegal moves that are also illegal according to the probe itself is very low (e.g., 0.023-0.051 for NT+JP models on R-10M/SF-8M/LC-16M), meaning the probe is often more accurate than the generative model's own predictions, further evidencing a weak causal link between probed board state and next-move prediction. Results are qualitatively robust to decoding policy: top-k=4 sampling (Table 5) increases ASR across the board relative to greedy decoding but preserves the same relative ranking of attacks, and notably IMO (designed assuming greedy decoding) remains the strongest attack even under sampling.
core_contribution: The paper (ICLR 2026, Balogh & Jelasity, U. Szeged) proposes 'adversarial sequence generation' as a methodology to test whether the implicit world model learned by a generative sequence model is SOUND with respect to a known ground-truth world model, using chess as the test domain. Rather than measuring next-token accuracy (shown by prior work to be misleading), the method has an adversary that only ever emits valid (legal) moves but chooses them so as to force the trained model's next prediction to be an illegal move. The paper formalizes soundness (Definition 2.1: model M with decoding policy m is sound w.r.t. true world model W iff for every valid sequence s with W(s) non-empty, m(s) in W(s)), introduces five adversary variants (IMO, BSO, AD, RM, SMM; see methodology), trains 24 GPT-2-scale (86M parameter) chess models across 6 datasets x 4 training objectives, and evaluates all combinations with all five attacks under both greedy and top-k decoding. It also separately investigates whether linear board-state probes have a causal role in next-token prediction, using gradient-alignment analysis and a targeted probe-vs-model-illegal-move overlap statistic.
threat_model: Not applicable — this is not a security paper. There is no adversary attacking a deployed system, no attacker/defender asymmetry, and no security property (confidentiality/integrity/availability, jailbreak, prompt injection, etc.) at stake. The word 'adversary'/'attack' is used in the interpretability/formal-verification sense (an adversarial *test generator* that searches for counterexamples to a soundness property), analogous to adversarial examples in robustness testing, not a security threat actor. The paper's own framing (Section 3, 'Adversarial Verification of Soundness') makes clear the goal is falsifying a formal soundness property of a generative model's implicit world model, not defeating a security control.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitation (Conclusions): the study relies on a single generative model architecture (GPT-2-style, 86M params) due to the cost of training/evaluating 24 model variants; effect of different architectures is left to future work, following the same limitation acknowledged in the cited prior works (Vafa et al. 2024; Li et al. 2023). The domain is restricted to chess, a fully deterministic, rule-based world model chosen for tractability; generalization to less formally specified 'world models' (e.g., natural language or code) is not tested. The formal soundness property only characterizes existence of at least one illegal continuation reachable via adversarial play; it does not quantify overall probability of illegality across the full distribution of play (the paper explicitly favors this because it avoids needing to define the generated formal language via a probability threshold, unlike Vafa et al. 2024, but this is a scope choice, not tested empirically against alternative formulations).

datasets_used: Millionbase-500K (500K high-quality human games, from Millionbase, per Toshniwal et al. 2022)
Stockfish-8M (8M games, Stockfish self-play vs engines of varying strength, per Karvonen 2024)
Lichess-16M (16M human games from the public Lichess database, per Karvonen 2024)
quantitative_results: Legal-move ratio on test set: 94.65%-96.71% for small datasets (Random-500K, Millionbase-500K) vs 99.75%-99.98% for large datasets (Random-10M, Stockfish-8M, Lichess-16M)
IMO attack success rate (greedy decoding, Table 1): near 1.00 on most models, e.g., 0.999-1.000 on Millionbase-500K, 0.972-0.992 on Random-10M, as low as 0.349-0.387 on Lichess-16M (NT/NT+JP)
AD (Vafa et al. 2024 reimplementation) vs RM baseline on Random-10M/NT: AD=0.516 vs RM=0.673 (AD comparable to or below random baseline)
BSO vs SMM baseline on Lichess-16M/NT: BSO=0.240 vs SMM=0.623 (BSO sometimes weaker than benign baseline)

OOD warmup effect on Lichess-16M/NT model, IMO attack (Table 4): success rate rises from 0.654 (in-distribution, Table 1) to 0.667 (+0.28), 0.785 (+0.40), 0.864 (+0.48) at 20/40/60 OOD moves respectively
Gradient cosine distance between next-token loss and board-state-probe loss (Table 2): 0.966-1.000 across all 24 models (near-orthogonal, i.e., near-independent subspaces)

Fraction of BSO-induced illegal moves that are also illegal per the probe's own board state (Table 3): as low as 0.023-0.051 for NT+JP models on large datasets (R-10M, SF-8M, LC-16M), indicating weak causal link
Top-k=4 sampling (Table 5) increases ASR across the board vs greedy decoding but preserves relative attack ranking; IMO remains strongest even under sampling
baselines_compared: Random Move (RM) — random legal move each step
Sequence Model Move (SMM) — benign oracle that plays the model's own top choice, correcting only illegal moves
Adversarial Detours (AD) — reimplementation of Vafa et al. (2024)'s lowest-probability legal-move attack, included specifically for comparison with that prior work
Illegal Move Oracle (IMO) — proposed attack maximizing probability of a subsequent illegal move
Board State Oracle (BSO) — proposed attack maximizing board-state probe loss
